channel.title: The Grant Desk - next 90 days
channel.description: Paid open calls, fellowships and residencies in AI, tech, research, and digital and mixed-media arts, sorted into the right pile. Updated as new calls land on the desk. Filtered to: next 90 days.

item.title: Freelands Foundation: Learning Through Making Fund
item.description:
Organisation: Freelands Foundation

Location: UK (England, Northern Ireland, Scotland, Wales); activity must occur within the United Kingdom.

Award: Up to £25,000. No application fee.

Duration: Projects of any length, taking place between 1 January and 31 December 2027. Funding provided no earlier than December 2026.

Deadline: 11 Sep 2026

The Learning Through Making fund supports UK-based visual art organisations to bring opportunities for making and material exploration into their art education work. Freelands Foundation is looking for exploratory projects that embrace openness, collaboration, and iteration -- enabling organisations to work with materials in an expansive way, unpicking preconceptions about making and material literacy without predefined outcomes. ELIGIBLE ACTIVITIES: providing materials, transport, or access to expertise and facilities; action-research trials such as a new series of workshops or events developing material literacy; adding a making-focused element to existing activity. Projects may range from a few days to several months, but must take place between 1 January and 31 December 2027. AMOUNT: up to £25,000; funding provided no earlier than December 2026. ELIGIBILITY: UK-based visual art organisations run with a charitable purpose and for public benefit; eligible types include charities registered in England and Wales, Scotland, or Northern Ireland; exempt charities; galleries, museums, or organisations within a Local Authority or larger charitable body (e.g. universities); and Community Interest Companies or Community Benefit Societies meeting specific criteria (charitable purposes, asset lock, no dividend distribution, ability to segregate restricted funds). Organisations with a prior Freelands Foundation grant relationship are eligible; you may not apply for activity already funded by Freelands Foundation. Individuals are not eligible. HOW TO APPLY: download the Application Form, Application Guidance, and optional Access Form from freelandsfoundation.co.uk; email completed form to grants@freelandsfoundation.co.uk with subject line 'LTM Fund - Application'; one additional page of images or diagrams may be included. APPLICATION SUPPORT SESSIONS (online): 9 July 2026, 10:30am-12pm; 12 August 2026, 10:30am-12pm. KEY DATES: applications open 17 June 2026; close 11 September 2026 at 12pm; outcomes shared 23 November 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: AI Research Fellow / Senior AI Policy Researcher (Utah Office of AI Policy)
item.description:
Organisation: Utah Department of Commerce, Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy

Location: Salt Lake City, Utah, United States. Hybrid telework may be available but regular in-person work is required, and in-state work rules require all work to be performed while physically within Utah. No visa sponsorship.

Award: Salary: USD 33.13 to 54.01 hourly wage. Full-time, time-limited position. Job classification Research Consultant III (#16048); may underfill as Research Consultant II (#16058) with a reduced salary range.

Duration: Full-time, time-limited (Schedule Code TL).

Deadline: 24 Jun 2026

The Utah Department of Commerce, Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy, is seeking an AI Research Fellow to help drive technical, scientific, and policy research for Utah's AI Learning Lab. This is an applied research and policy-translation role whose work should directly inform regulatory decisions, legislative recommendations, regulatory mitigation agreements, and stakeholder strategy. The role is technical and relies heavily on statistical, machine learning, computer science, and empirical research skills, while also requiring strong judgment, clear writing, and the ability to operate in a small multidisciplinary team alongside colleagues with policy, business, regulatory, and legal expertise. The Office of Artificial Intelligence Policy was created in May 2024 to systematically develop sensible AI policy across major impact areas such as healthcare, education, finance, transportation, and consumer technology. Central to the Office is the AI Learning Lab, where industry experts, academics, regulators, and policymakers work toward appropriate regulatory responses to emerging AI applications; the Office also has authority to create regulatory mitigation agreements that let responsible AI businesses test products in Utah under structured conditions while the state gathers evidence. KEY RESPONSIBILITIES: independently scope and execute research projects on emerging AI policy questions (defining the question, identifying technical and empirical evidence, interviewing experts, evaluating tradeoffs, and producing written recommendations); research the technical aspects, capabilities, limitations, and societal impacts of AI technologies; evaluate evidence and forecasts on the impact of human-AI interactions on health, economic activity, education, consumer welfare, and public institutions; draft executive policy memos, legislative briefings, technical explainers, risk assessments, and regulatory mitigation analyses; interface with business leaders, technologists, researchers, regulators, legislators, and lawyers; and advise on the development of regulatory and legal standards. MINIMUM QUALIFICATIONS: deep technical knowledge of statistical methods, machine learning, and AI systems; strong ability to translate technical and empirical evidence into practical policy options for nontechnical decision-makers; familiarity with human-subject research, qualitative and quantitative methods, medical or health sciences, social sciences, human-computer interaction, or related empirical fields; excellent problem-solving, reliability, and independent judgment; and strong technical communication across policy, legal, regulatory, business, and technical audiences. PREFERRED: experience implementing, evaluating, or deploying AI systems (business, healthcare, government, or other applied settings); expertise in public policy, law, regulatory design, or government decision-making; ability to evaluate technical claims, research methods, and evidence quality; specific healthcare experience; and experience supporting AI deployment in regulated environments. A criminal history check is required. Recruiter: Tehra Gorski, tgorski@utah.gov, 801-530-6030. Application deadline 24 June 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: AY-ROOP / Le Milieu: Reuse - (Re)making for Circus Residency 2026 (Rennes Métropole, France)
item.description:
Organisation: AY-ROOP

Location: Le Milieu, Saint-Jacques-de-la-Lande, Rennes Métropole, France. Applicants must legally reside in a Creative Europe country other than France and the French overseas territories (DROM-COM).

Award: Funded through Culture Moves Europe: EUR 900 mobility allowance per person plus flat-rate travel coverage; supplements for green mobility, visa fees, dependent children under 18, OCT/OR residents, and disability/accessibility needs (including possible full coverage of a sign-language interpreter). On-site single-room accommodation, lunches at Le Milieu canteen (Tuesday to Friday), shared kitchen, and technical support provided. No application fee.

Duration: 21-day residency, 28 November to 18 December 2026.

Deadline: 03 Jul 2026

AY-ROOP is launching an open call to select three circus artists and/or circus set designers/builders for a 21-day Culture Moves Europe research residency at Le Milieu (Saint-Jacques-de-la-Lande, Rennes Métropole) from 28 November to 18 December 2026. The residency is a research laboratory on reuse in contemporary circus: how everyday, locally available materials (paper/cardboard, plastics, lightweight metal, textiles, reclaimed wood, bicycle parts, etc.) can be used to create scenographic elements, sets, props and small apparatuses for juggling, acrobatics and balancing. The core principle is that objects are recreated locally from resources available in each territory rather than transported. AY-ROOP provides an equipped construction workshop, a rehearsal stage and circus tent, a guide to local reuse-sector contacts, and a full residency environment (accommodation, shared kitchen, working facilities); two mentors (a reuse specialist and a performing-arts professional) support the artists. PROFILES: circus artists interested in construction/scenography/DIY making, and builders/set designers working in circus; individual or group applications (max 3 people per group); emerging or established, any background. Accessible to artists with disabilities and accessibility needs. Working language English. No aerial apparatus or rigging: all work is ground-based. ELIGIBILITY: aged 18+ (no upper limit); legally resident in a Creative Europe country other than France and DROM-COM; available for the full residency. APPLY with a single PDF (1-page bio/CV, 5-page portfolio or video links, 1-page statement of intent, proof of residence). DEADLINE: 3 July 2026. Results announced 13 July 2026. Check the linked call PDF for full details.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: NEW INC Year 13 Mentor Open Call
item.description:
Organisation: NEW INC (New Museum Incubator)

Location: New York, NY, USA; primarily in-person at 250 Hudson St, lower Manhattan. Some hybrid participation possible.

Award: Dedicated Mentors: $500 honorarium. Office Hours Mentors: $300 honorarium. All mentors also receive a one-year All Access Plus membership to the New Museum. No application fee.

Duration: Year 13 program (September 2026 onward); average 4-8 hours of engagement per week.

Deadline: 08 Jul 2026

NEW INC, the New Museum's cultural incubator, is seeking mentors for Year 13 of its program supporting creative practitioners at the intersection of art, design, and technology. This is a call for mentors (creative and business leaders), not for program members. TWO MENTOR TYPES: Dedicated Mentors ($500 honorarium) are matched one-on-one with a member and commit to ongoing support throughout the year; Office Hours Mentors ($300 honorarium) offer one-on-one sessions to the broader community on an as-needed basis. All mentors receive a one-year All Access Plus membership to the New Museum, invitations to special events including the annual DEMO Festival, and access to community-only workshops, talks, and social events. YEAR 13 PROGRAM TRACKS mentors may support: Art & Code; Creative Science; Extended Realities; Launchpad; Social Architecture. KEY DATES: Mentor Open Call closes 8 July 2026; Mentor Open House (virtual info session) 17 June 2026, 12-1pm ET (register via the site); mandatory Dedicated Mentor matchmaking ranking session 11 September 2026; mandatory Dedicated Mentor matchmaking event 18 September 2026; Dedicated Mentor matches announced 2 October 2026. Most NEW INC activities take place Monday-Friday, 10am-6pm ET. Apply at https://www.newmuseum.org/new-inc/open-call/.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: OIF: Support for Artist Mobility and Circulation of Cultural Goods 2026 (Francophonie)
item.description:
Organisation: Organisation internationale de la Francophonie (OIF)

Location: Francophone zone (OIF member states globally)

Award: Up to EUR 5,000 for artist mobility (travel, accommodation, visas, logistics). Up to EUR 2,500 for circulation of cultural goods (excess baggage, freight, customs). Both can be combined. No application fee.

Duration: Residencies or tours of 5 days to 3 months. Projects must take place between 25 March and 30 November 2026.

Deadline: 31 Aug 2026

The OIF Support for Artist Mobility and Circulation of Cultural Goods programme provides grants of up to EUR 5,000 for mobility (travel, accommodation, visa fees, logistics) and up to EUR 2,500 for circulation of cultural goods (excess baggage, freight, customs) within the Francophone zone; both can be combined. ELIGIBILITY: professional artists and established artistic collectives with a demonstrated connection to the Francophone space (by nationality, working language, or artistic collaborations), residing in an OIF member state, with minimum 2 years of experience in the cultural or artistic sector; must directly manage the project (not act as intermediaries); one application per individual per call. ELIGIBLE DISCIPLINES: live performance (music, theatre, dance, comedy, storytelling), cinema/audiovisual, literature/publishing, and visual arts. PRIORITY given to projects promoting gender equality, women artists, creators under 35, and South-South mobility. Projects must take place between 25 March and 30 November 2026. Multiple selection sessions throughout the year; call open until 31 August 2026. Information webinars: 11 March, 13 May, and 8 July 2026 at 3 PM Paris time. Contact: culture.dlc@francophonie.org. Apply at https://appelsaprojets.francophonie.org/fr/applications/soutien-a-la-circulation-des-biens-et-mobilite-des-artistes.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Canon Foundation in Europe Research Fellowships 2026 (Europe–Japan Exchange)
item.description:
Organisation: Canon Foundation in Europe

Location: Europe or Japan (bilateral exchange; fellows arrange own host institution)

Award: Up to EUR 30,000 per year (pro-rated for shorter stays). No application fee.

Duration: Minimum 3 months, maximum 1 year. Fellowships for 2027 start.

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

Canon Foundation in Europe Research Fellowships support bilateral research exchange between Europe and Japan, awarding up to 15 fellowships annually. AMOUNT: up to EUR 30,000 per year (pro-rated for shorter stays), flexible — covers living costs, travel, insurance, research expenses, and books; fellows determine their own allocation. ELIGIBILITY: European nationals (including UK, Israel, Switzerland, Turkey, Balkan and Baltic countries) conducting research in Japan; or Japanese nationals conducting research in Europe. Applicants must have completed at least a Master's or PhD degree within the last 10 years (older qualifications may apply with supporting documentation). No restrictions on research field. Priority given to first-time applicants to Europe/Japan. Fellows arrange their own host institution and advisor. Up to 15 fellowships awarded annually. Deadline: 15 September 2026, 23:59 CET (for fellowships starting January-December 2027). Apply at https://portal.canonfoundation.org/. Contact: canon.foundation@canon-europe.com.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Fulbright-Iméra Mediterranean Studies Award 2027-2028 (Aix-Marseille, US Faculty)
item.description:
Organisation: Iméra – Aix Marseille Université, in partnership with the Fulbright Commission

Location: Aix-Marseille, France (campus residency)

Award: €2,400 (~USD 2,600) monthly stipend; fully covered accommodation (valued USD 788–1,966/month); €1,200 (~USD 1,300) travel allowance. No application fee.

Duration: 5 or 10 months (September 2027 – June 2028, flexible start/end dates).

Deadline: 15 Sep 2026

The Fulbright-Iméra Mediterranean Studies Award supports US faculty to conduct research at the Iméra Institute for Advanced Study at Aix Marseille Université. ELIGIBILITY: mid-career, senior, or community college faculty affiliated with a US institution of higher education; US citizens residing outside France (permanent residents of France are ineligible); any discipline relating to Mediterranean studies; English sufficient (French desirable). 1 or 2 grants awarded. PROVIDED: €2,400/month stipend; fully equipped apartment with linens; office in co-working space; visa fees waived; academic event organisation expenses covered; school placement assistance for dependents. DURATION: 5 or 10 months (September 2027 – June 2028, flexible). DEADLINE: 15 September 2026. Apply via the Fulbright Scholar Program at https://fulbrightscholars.org/award/fulbright-imera-mediterranean-studies-award-aix-marseille-university.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Center for Humane Technology: Emerging Voices in AI and Society Fellowship (Inaugural Cohort)
item.description:
Organisation: Center for Humane Technology (CHT)

Location: Remote, 10 hours/week overlapping with EST required.

Award: $30,000 fixed stipend paid in three milestone-based tranches. No application fee.

Duration: 6 months: September 14, 2026 through March 18, 2027. 15 hours per week.

Deadline: 12 Jul 2026

CHT's inaugural Emerging Voices in AI and Society Fellowship places 3 fellows (one per focus area) in a 6-month, part-time program to develop rigorous public-impact research and writing on how AI is reshaping society. Fellows choose from 5 focus areas: (1) AI and Cognition — what AI does to human judgment, critical thinking, and intellectual independence; (2) AI and Relationships — AI's effects on intimacy, companionship, and social bonds; (3) AI and Surveillance — how AI enables monitoring, tracking, and control; (4) AI and Spirituality — AI's intersection with meaning, transcendence, and religious practice; (5) Inside the Machine: AI From a Technologist's Perspective. Up to 2 focus areas per application. DELIVERABLES: one flagship research piece, report, or long-form essay for public impact; 1-3 op-eds (placed in target outlets or CHT Substack); one podcast appearance (Your Undivided Attention or equivalent); cohort meetings and bi-weekly check-ins. SUPPORT: bi-weekly guidance from Senior Director of Strategy and Impact; editorial, communications and graphic design support; media training and PR placement support; CHT email address and Slack access; listing on CHT's public fellows page. WHO SHOULD APPLY: mid-career researchers (psychology, sociology, philosophy, cognitive science, communications, STS), policy analysts, journalists covering tech and society, civil society advocates, or technologists with insider perspective. Strong preference for those with a public writing track record (op-eds, Substack, peer-reviewed work) and some public presence. HOW TO APPLY: 1-2 page research proposal per topic (up to 2), CV/resume (2 pages max), 2-minute video explaining fit, writing sample. Deadline: Sunday 12 July 2026, 11:59pm EST.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: European Commission: Safeguarding Europe's Born-Digital Heritage (PPPA-2026-BORN-DIGITAL)
item.description:
Organisation: European Commission (Pilot Projects and Preparatory Actions, Culture strand)

Location: European Union. Consortium of at least 5 independent entities from 5 different EU member states required.

Award: Up to €1,985,000, covering up to 85% of eligible project costs (15% co-financing required). No application fee.

Duration: Project duration to be defined in the proposal; covers a scoping and mapping phase across EU member states.

Deadline: 16 Jul 2026

The European Commission is offering a single grant of up to €1,985,000 (85% co-financing) for a project focused on safeguarding born-digital cultural heritage across EU member states. Scope includes: assessing the preservation state of born-digital art, web archives, video games, and digital content created outside cultural institutions; mapping EU and national legal frameworks; identifying non-legislative barriers (technical, financial, organisational, cultural); and providing guidance on creating preservation copies of licensed or legally accessible materials. This is a scoping and advocacy project, not a digitisation grant. ELIGIBILITY: the application must be led by a consortium of at least 5 independent entities from 5 different EU countries, which rules out solo applicants and single-country organisations. Grant reference: PPPA-2026-BORN-DIGITAL. Deadline: 16 July 2026. Verify the current deadline at ec.europa.eu before applying (one secondary source listed 1 June 2026; the primary call-fiche PDF and the Cyprus government funding portal both confirm 16 July 2026).

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Kometa Fellows 2026: Field Journalism, Photography, and Writing
item.description:
Organisation: Kometa (French literary and journalism magazine)

Location: Remote / field-based (international)

Award: €10,000 per Fellow, six Fellows selected. No application fee.

Duration: One year of funding for field work and research

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

Kometa, a French literary and journalism magazine, awards fellowships to journalists, writers, and photographers documenting reality in contexts of war, censorship, or severe social and political tensions. Projects must be rooted in direct field experience and focused on contemporary rather than historical events. Projects already under a contractual agreement are ineligible. AMOUNT: €10,000 per Fellow, six Fellows selected. WHAT FELLOWS RECEIVE: one year of funding; publication of the completed project (partial or full) in Kometa; opportunity to contribute to the monthly newsletter; mentorship through editorial collaboration. 2026 FOCUS AREAS: disinformation and narrative wars; political and social changes in the Gulf. DEADLINE: 31 July 2026. APPLY via the Tally form (https://tally.so/r/J9XxMK), in French or English (about 30 to 45 minutes). Contact: fellows@kometarevue.com.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Simone Veil Prize for European Jewish Cultural Heritage 2026
item.description:
Organisation: European Commission, Directorate-General for Education, Youth, Sport and Culture

Location: EU Member States and third countries associated to the Creative Europe programme

Award: €10,000 per Grand Prix winner (five winners, one per category); up to 25 finalists recognized. No application fee.

Duration: For projects completed between January 2023 and January 2026

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

The Simone Veil Prize, administered by the European Commission, recognises outstanding projects preserving and promoting European Jewish cultural heritage. It is awarded across five categories: Shared Histories, Heritage for Dialogue, Living Heritage, Cross-border Cooperation and Shared Narratives, and Next Generation/Youth Engagement and Education. AWARD: €10,000 per Grand Prix winner (one per category, five in total); up to 25 finalists receive recognition. ELIGIBILITY: legal or natural persons (individuals or organisations) registered in EU Member States or third countries associated to the Creative Europe programme; projects must have been completed between January 2023 and January 2026. DEADLINE: 31 July 2026 at 23:59 CET. APPLY by submitting the completed application form by email to applications@simoneveilprize.eu.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Lee Ufan Arles: Prix Art & Environnement 2026 (Residency + Exhibition Prize)
item.description:
Organisation: Lee Ufan Arles and Maison Guerlain

Location: Arles, France (residency and exhibition)

Award: Funded residency (production space + accommodation) in Arles. No cash prize amount specified. No application fee.

Duration: 6–8 weeks residency in early 2027, followed by a solo summer 2027 exhibition

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

The Prix Art & Environnement (4th edition), awarded by Lee Ufan Arles and Maison Guerlain, supports an artist working at the intersection of art and the natural environment. The prize winner receives a funded 6–8 week production residency in Arles in early 2027, including production space, accommodation, and mentorship from Lee Ufan Arles staff, culminating in a solo summer 2027 exhibition. ELIGIBILITY: open to all artists of any discipline and nationality, with no age or degree requirements; candidates must demonstrate at least one solo exhibition and one or more group exhibitions. DEADLINE: 31 July 2026. APPLY via the Google Form linked from the Lee Ufan Arles website.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Longview Philanthropy: Digital Minds Research Fellowships 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Longview Philanthropy

Location: Worldwide / remote or institutional (grant-eligible countries listed in application form)

Award: $55,000–$120,000/year through a university, or $80,000–$150,000/year direct stipend; plus research budget (10% of salary), conference travel, and compute resources where applicable. No application fee.

Duration: 1–2 years. Second year conditional on continuing to meet programme requirements.

Deadline: 10 Jul 2026

Longview Philanthropy Research Fellowships support scholars pursuing research on the potential consciousness, sentience, moral status, and legal position of AI systems. AMOUNT: $55,000–$120,000/year (through a university, with 10% research budget and standard fringe benefits), or $80,000–$150,000/year (direct stipend, with suggested 5–10% allocation for research and travel). Compute resources available for machine learning research. Invitation and funding to attend the annual Center for Mind, Ethics, and Policy Summit at NYU. ELIGIBILITY: must have or be expected to receive a doctoral degree in computer science, neuroscience, a related technical discipline, law, medicine, or applied social science by September 2027 or fellowship start date (exceptions for equivalent research experience); fellows arrange their own institutional affiliation; fellowship may be conditional on institutional affiliation or attendance at events. SELECTION CRITERIA: demonstrated interest in digital minds and its relation to AI risk; evidence of research promise (publications, academic achievements, references). DURATION: 1–2 years; second year conditional on ongoing programme requirements. AREAS OF INTEREST: introspection, welfare measurement, interpretability, model character, correlates of consciousness, valence research, governance frameworks, legal and political responses to potentially sentient AI. Deadline: 10 July 2026. Required application materials: CV, description of possible research directions (~1,000 words), intended outcomes, what you would do without this fellowship (~150–400 words), career goals (~300 words). Questions: digitalminds-rfp@longview.org. Apply at https://www.longview.org/request-for-proposals-research-and-applied-work-on-digital-minds/.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Híbrides: Small Embodied Data - Call for Contributions (2nd Edition)
item.description:
Organisation: Axolot.cat collective and DARTS research group, Universitat Oberta de Catalunya (UOC)

Location: Barcelona, Spain; UOC Poblenou campus and Convent de Sant Agustí. In-person attendance required.

Award: Oral presentations: €100 honorarium (invoice required). Performances: €400 honorarium (invoice required). No submission fee.

Duration: Festival runs 26-28 November 2026.

Deadline: 07 Sep 2026

Híbrides is a biennial of artistic research co-organised by the Axolot.cat collective and the DARTS (Design, Art, Technology and Society) research group at the UOC in Barcelona. The second edition, "Small Embodied Data," centres on the concept of classification -- approached through machine learning, data science, algorithmic systems, and questions of who designs classifiers, under what ethical codes, and to what end. The biennial brings classification into dialogue with its previous themes of Small Data (community-defined, intentional data collection and analysis) and Embodiment (bodily experience as a foundation of situated, sensory knowledge). Both academic and non-academic contributions are welcome. FORMATS ACCEPTED: Oral Presentations (20 minutes; artistic practice, artistic research, or academic research projects; may be video-recorded for a public archive and developed into short papers); Performances (20-30 minutes; must engage with the physical body, stage, and technology; complete works, including previously presented work; takes place in a multipurpose space with technical support). SUBMISSION REQUIREMENTS: online form with abstract (max 1,000 characters), professional biography (max 1,000 characters), and link to representative materials (3-5 images; video and technical rider required for performances). HONORARIA: oral presentations €100; performances €400; invoice required in both cases. Participants must follow the event's code of conduct. Event runs 26-28 November 2026 at UOC Poblenou campus and Convent de Sant Agustí, Barcelona. Deadline: 7 September 2026. Submit at https://hibrides.axolot.cat/opencall.html.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: AdK / EMS Berlin-Stockholm Bilateral Residency Program 2026-27
item.description:
Organisation: Studio für Elektroakustische Musik, Akademie der Künste (Berlin) and Elektronmusikstudion EMS (Stockholm)

Location: Berlin, Germany and Stockholm, Sweden; travel and accommodation covered for stays in both cities.

Award: Travel and accommodation costs for stays in Berlin and Stockholm covered; fees paid for presentation at KONTAKTE Festival 2027 (Berlin) and for presentation in Stockholm. Amount not publicly stated. No application fee.

Duration: Flexible, by agreement; works to be presented at KONTAKTE 2027 in Berlin and at a Stockholm presentation.

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

A new bilateral residency collaboration between the Studio für Elektroakustische Musik at the Akademie der Künste in Berlin and Elektronmusikstudion EMS in Stockholm. Four artists will be selected to develop and present new works in both cities, with presentations at KONTAKTE 2027 in Berlin and at a corresponding event in Stockholm. The program supports new artistic and technical initiatives at the intersection of sound, music, and technology in the field of time-based, performative, and digital arts. ELIGIBLE CATEGORIES: (1) new instrumental and live-electronic works; (2) acousmatic music, computer music, radio art, and other forms of electroacoustic music and sound art; (3) projects from artists of any discipline for whom sound is a central element; (4) curatorial, publishing, and educational projects in sound-based art practice, particularly those relating in content to the AdK Studio or EMS; (5) independent research in acoustics, computer science, or musicology -- specifically software development, spatialization, or network technologies. Open to individuals and groups. Project duration is flexible and arranged by agreement. SUPPORT: travel and accommodation costs for Berlin and Stockholm covered; presentation fees paid for both venues. Selection is made by the director and advisory board of the Studio für Elektroakustische Musik and the director of EMS; decision is final with no legal entitlement to funding. Incomplete or late applications are not considered. This call supplements the Studio's regular residency program (separate deadline June 30, 2026 for July-December 2026 period). Apply exclusively via the online form at https://nc.adk.de/index.php/apps/forms/s/HD2nxbzqwgeMHWN6xHg9bQrA. Questions: estudio@adk.de. Deadline: 31 July 2026.

Open call details

item.title: Democracy Stories Lab: Open Call for Visual Storytellers
item.description:
Organisation: People Powered and Democracy Narratives Alliance

Location: Worldwide; remote. Global screening event for winners.

Award: USD $1,000 for each of 4 winners; USD $500 for each of 5 honorable mentions. Plus amplification across People Powered and Democracy Narratives Alliance networks and feature in a global screening event. No application fee.

Duration: One-time award.

Deadline: 14 Sep 2026

The Democracy Stories Lab, an initiative of People Powered (a global hub for participatory democracy) in partnership with the Democracy Narratives Alliance, invites creators worldwide to submit visual content -- short videos, illustrations, or graphics -- that tells a compelling story about democracy, voice, fairness, belonging, and community power. Content can be original or already published, as long as it is the applicant's own work and engages with narrative frameworks from the Democracy Narratives Alliance's research on 'How to Talk About Democracy.' Tone is open; serious and comedic approaches are both welcome. AWARDS: 4 winning entries receive USD $1,000 each; 5 honorable mentions receive USD $500 each; all winners receive amplification across People Powered and Democracy Narratives Alliance communications networks and are featured in a global screening event. WHO CAN APPLY: individual creators and small teams anywhere in the world; especially welcomes creators who reach audiences outside usual democracy circles, who come from communities traditionally with less of a say in public discourse, and who work in civic tech, advocacy, community organising, comedy, art, illustration, games, or lifestyle content. Applications open 14 July 2026 via the Democracy Stories Lab page at https://www.peoplepowered.org/news-content/announcing-the-democracy-stories-lab; subscribe to the People Powered newsletter to be notified when the form opens. Submission deadline: 14 September 2026. Winners announced October-November 2026. Note: the initiative was itself voted into existence through People Powered's participatory budgeting process (PP Decides).

Open call details

item.title: Stichting Stokroos: Residency at Fundaziun Nairs 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Stichting Stokroos

Location: Residency in Scuol, Switzerland (Fundaziun Nairs). Applicants must live and work in the Kingdom of the Netherlands (including its Caribbean parts) or Suriname for at least one year. Applicants outside the Randstad particularly encouraged.

Award: Fully supported stay (accommodation and meals paid directly to Fundaziun Nairs) plus an unrestricted living, travel and production budget of EUR 7,000. 80% paid before the residency, the remainder on receipt of a brief final report. No application fee.

Duration: Intensive three-month working period (July through September).

Deadline: 11 Sep 2026

Stichting Stokroos funds a three-month reflective residency at Fundaziun Nairs in Scuol, Switzerland, for one selected visual artist, designer or craft practitioner (overlap possible). Both emerging and established makers may apply. Stokroos covers full accommodation and meals directly with Fundaziun Nairs and provides a discretionary EUR 7,000 budget for living, travel and production. The application should include a proposal that engages specifically with the residency, its Alpine surroundings and/or its historical and cultural context. Eligibility: you completed your first subject-specific (vocational or higher) programme in 2022 or earlier, or have an equivalent established practice, or started without formal training (first presentation marks the start of your practice); at least 21 years old; living and working in the Kingdom of the Netherlands (including its Caribbean parts) or Suriname for at least one year; not currently enrolled in a study or post-academic programme. Applications are individual only; duos and collectives cannot apply. Makers already selected by Fundaziun Nairs may still apply if they meet the criteria. You may submit only one application per year across all Stokroos grants, and must not have received a Seed Grant rejection in the previous calendar year. Apply via the online form (Dutch or English) with a portfolio PDF up to 20 pages, optional video up to 10 minutes total, CV up to 2 pages, and work/plan descriptions up to 2,500 characters each. Selection includes an introductory interview with up to three candidates; Stokroos aims to respond within 8 weeks. No appeal is possible. Application window: 1 to 11 September 2026. More information at nairs.ch.

Open call details

item.title: Longview Philanthropy: Digital Minds Grants for Applied Work 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Longview Philanthropy

Location: Worldwide / remote (grant-eligible countries listed in application form)

Award: Typical grants $50,000–$2,000,000 per project; proposals outside this range considered when the work justifies it. May include some operational support for new organisations. No application fee.

Duration: Project-based; duration varies by scope.

Deadline: 10 Jul 2026

Longview Philanthropy invites proposals for applied work on the potential consciousness, sentience, moral status, or experiences of AI systems. Typical grants are $50,000–$2,000,000 per project, with proposals outside that range considered when justified. New organisations may also receive some operational support. ELIGIBLE ACTIVITIES: seed funding for new organisations (think tanks, research bodies, initiatives); mentorship programmes for researchers entering the digital minds field; internships, new hires, or focused programmes on digital minds at existing AI organisations, academic labs, or policy institutions; convenings and field-building (workshops, conferences, summits, working groups addressing technical and governance questions). AREAS OF INTEREST: measuring AI welfare; consciousness and sentience research; governance and legal/social science frameworks for potentially sentient AI; communications work for thoughtful public discourse; research beyond the US; support organisations and operations professionals for independent technical researchers. SELECTION CRITERIA: potential impact, track record, demonstrated interest in digital minds and its relationship to AI risk, cost-effectiveness, legal compliance. Deadline: 10 July 2026. Questions: digitalminds-rfp@longview.org. Apply at https://www.longview.org/request-for-proposals-research-and-applied-work-on-digital-minds/.

Open call details

item.title: Revitalizing Rituals 2026 Open Call
item.description:
Organisation: Homo Faber Alapítvány (Homo Faber Foundation)

Location: Bhar Lazreg, Tunisia and Somogy County, Hungary; multi-stage in-person residency across both sites.

Award: EUR 6,000 gross artist fee and production budget; international travel and per diems covered; on-site accommodation provided. No application fee.

Duration: Multi-stage residency: Placemaking Camp (1 week, November 2026, Hungary); Storytellers Camp (1 week, March 2027, Tunisia); Residency Period (minimum 1 month, April-July 2027); public presentation (August 2027); Closing Event (3 days, winter/early spring 2028, Hungary).

Deadline: 19 Jul 2026

Revitalizing Rituals invites artists from all disciplines -- sound, movement, visual arts, installation, performance, and transdisciplinary practices -- to join a research-creation journey across two landscapes: Bhar Lazreg in Tunisia and the wetlands of Somogy County in Hungary. The project explores rituals as living forms of belonging: reconnecting communities, land, memory, food, ecological reciprocity, and the more-than-human world. Selected artists work with local communities as co-participants in a shared inquiry into moving from extraction toward reciprocity. Organised by Homo Faber Alapítvány (Hungary). AMOUNT: EUR 6,000 gross artist fee and production budget; international travel and per diems covered; on-site accommodation provided. ELIGIBILITY: minimum 5 years of active artistic practice; portfolio reflecting interest in situated or relational work; no formal degree required; working language is English (local language navigation valued); must be available for the full residency cycle including all international travel; must be able to legally receive the fee and invoice the organising partners from country of residence. Strongly encourages applications from historically marginalised or underrepresented voices. KEY DATES: Application Deadline 19 July 2026 (midnight) -- note the open call PDF also references a 5 July date; the officially promoted deadline is 19 July; Online Q&A 29 June 2026, 14:00 Tunisia / 15:00 CET (register at https://forms.gle/brnZvXvFe4JB8zL26); Online Conversations with shortlisted artists: mid July to mid August 2026; Selection Results: 31 August 2026; Placemaking Camp: 1 week, end of November 2026, Hungary; Storytellers Camp: 1 week, March 2027, Tunisia; Residency Period: minimum 1 month (splittable), April to July 2027; Final Presentation: August 2027; Closing Event: 3 days, winter/early spring 2028, Hungary. Apply via the open call form linked at https://homofaber.hu/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/Artist-Open-Call-FINAL-in-the-RR-form.pdf.

Open call details

item.title: The Wheeler Centre: The Next Chapter 2027
item.description:
Organisation: The Wheeler Centre

Location: Melbourne, Australia; includes residency at Varuna, The National Writers' House, and an end-of-year publishing intensive at The Wheeler Centre.

Award: AUD $15,000 stipend per fellow. No application fee.

Duration: 12 months.

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

The Next Chapter is The Wheeler Centre's flagship writing program, designed to elevate Australian stories that are underrepresented and to nurture a new wave of writers to tell them. Each fellow receives a AUD $15,000 stipend and is paired with a handpicked mentor for a twelve-month period to progress a major writing project. Fellows also receive a residency at Varuna, The National Writers' House; an end-of-year publishing intensive at The Wheeler Centre; and meaningful opportunities to connect with peers, agents, publishers, and readers. One fellowship place is dedicated specifically to a poet: The Malcolm Robertson Fellowship. 2027 judges: Melissa Lucashenko, Micaela Sahhar, Cher Tan, and Melanie Saward. ELIGIBILITY: Australian writers. No specific genre restrictions stated; the program focuses on stories that are not currently being told and voices that are underrepresented. TIMELINE: submissions open 2 June 2026; submissions close 31 July 2026 (5pm); 2027 Next Chapter Fellows announced 10 November 2026. Questions: thenextchapter@wheelercentre.com. Apply at https://www.wheelercentre.com/the-next-chapter.

Open call details

item.title: Jamverse Jam (Protocolized worldbuilding contest)
item.description:
Organisation: Protocolized (Summer of Protocols)

Location: Online / Worldwide

Award: One USD 1,000 grand prize plus at least 10 additional USD 200 prizes; winning artifacts are published on the Jamverse website. No entry fee.

Duration: One-off contest; submit up to three artifacts by 31 July 2026

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

Protocolized's fourth contest invites artists and writers (storytellers, poets, comic artists) to build, extend, and connect the Jamverse, a shared near-future science fiction universe of four intersecting story cycles (The Stockton Chronicles, Legends and Ledgers, UET-Trainverse, and Zoothesia). ELIGIBILITY: open to anyone; you may submit up to three in-universe worldbuilding artifacts, and the same creator can win multiple prizes. REQUIREMENTS: each artifact must be under 1,000 words (200-700 is the sweet spot) or a visual equivalent (comic panel, poster, advertisement, etc.) and must include at least one element drawn from one of the four sub-world 'toy boxes', a surprising 'traffic jam' (an emergent, ah-ha consequence of a technology or protocol), and a relation to the theme of Stigmergy (indirect coordination through modifying a shared environment). Artifact types can include comic panels, job postings, poems, forum threads, historic documents, or encyclopedia entries. APPLY: submit each artifact as a public Google Doc via the contest form, including the artifact, a one-paragraph explanation of the worlds/toy-box components used and how the piece relates to stigmergy and explores a traffic jam, and, if AI was used, documentation of your AI/LLM protocol. PRIZES: one USD 1,000 grand prize and at least 10 USD 200 prizes, all published on the Jamverse website. DEADLINE: 31 July 2026. Contest rules, the current Jamverse stories, and starter materials are on the Jamverse website.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: TBA21-Academy: Tentacular Fellowship 2026-2027 (Ocean Space, Venice)
item.description:
Organisation: TBA21-Academy, with NICHE Centre for Environmental Humanities and the UNESCO Chair on Water, Heritage and Sustainable Development at Ca' Foscari University

Location: Ocean Space and Ca' Foscari University, Venice, Italy; situated work in and with the communities of the Lagoon of Venice.

Award: Total fellowship support EUR 26,000. Phase 1 (incubation): EUR 3,000 grant for independent research and initiative development. Phase 2 (activation): EUR 8,000 grant plus a EUR 15,000 activation budget. No application fee.

Duration: 15 months, September 2026 to November 2027

Deadline: 05 Jul 2026

TBA21-Academy, in collaboration with NICHE Centre for Environmental Humanities and the UNESCO Chair on Water, Heritage and Sustainable Development at Ca' Foscari University, is launching the first edition of the Tentacular Fellowship, an initiative-building fellowship based at Ocean Space and at Ca' Foscari in Venice. Conceived as a long-term platform for artistic research and ecological engagement, Ocean Space works to develop new forms of action capable of moving beyond awareness-raising to produce real, tangible territorial effects through situated collaboration with the communities, practitioners, researchers and civic actors already working within the Lagoon of Venice. The fellowship invites a practitioner or collective to initiate, consolidate or expand a situated initiative in the Lagoon through the creation of a Tentacular Unit: a collaborative, interdisciplinary platform able to gather artists, students, researchers, institutions, local communities and other actors around a specific socio-environmental challenge. Through situated research, alliance-building and public-facing formats, the fellow helps build the social, institutional and ecological conditions for the initiative to take root and grow over time. STRUCTURE: the two-year fellowship runs in two phases. In Phase 1 (incubation) the fellow is awarded a EUR 3,000 grant to support independent research and initiative development. In Phase 2 (activation) the fellow is awarded a EUR 8,000 grant plus a EUR 15,000 activation budget, for total fellowship support of EUR 26,000. Details on the application process, evaluation criteria and frequently asked questions are in the full open-call document. Application deadline: 5 July 2026, 11:59pm CET. Apply via the online form linked from the open-call page.

Open call details

item.title: Stichting Stokroos: Maker Initiatives Grant 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Stichting Stokroos

Location: Kingdom of the Netherlands (including its Caribbean parts) or Suriname; must have lived and worked there at least one year. Applicants outside the Randstad, or plans taking place there, are particularly encouraged.

Award: Fixed grant of EUR 25,000 or EUR 40,000 (chosen depending on need). Maximum participant fee EUR 2,200/month (40-hour week); third-party hire capped at EUR 85/hour excl. VAT. Co-funding not required. No application fee.

Duration: Plan starts in the next calendar year (2027) and runs a maximum of two years.

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

Stichting Stokroos Maker Initiatives supports established visual artists, designers and craft practitioners (overlap possible), including duos and collectives, in developing and realising a multivocal artistic project or programme centred on a common concern, in collaboration with other makers or communities. There is one application round per year and Stokroos can support 4 to 6 proposals; a maximum of 40 eligible applications are considered per period. Eligibility: you completed your first subject-specific (vocational or higher) programme in 2020 or earlier, or have an equivalent established track record, or started without formal training (first presentation marks the start of your practice); at least 21 years old; living and working in the Kingdom of the Netherlands (including its Caribbean parts) or Suriname for at least one year; not currently enrolled in a study or post-academic programme. You must apply together with an artistic or creative partner (from professional makers to communities); duos and collectives must also have an external partner. You may submit only one application per year across all Stokroos grants, and must not have received a Seed Grant rejection in the previous calendar year. The grant is a fixed contribution of EUR 25,000 or EUR 40,000 depending on need. Plans can take place anywhere, start in the next calendar year and last a maximum of two years. Applications are submitted via the online form in Dutch or English (portfolio PDF up to 20 pages/person, optional video up to 10 minutes total, CV up to 2 pages/person, budget up to 3 pages, work/plan/collaboration descriptions up to 2,500 characters each). Stokroos aims to respond within 10 weeks; no appeal is possible. Application window: 20 to 31 July 2026. Questions: mail@stokroos.nl.

item.title: Pioneer Works: 2027 Open Call – Visual Arts and Music Residency (Brooklyn, NY)
item.description:
Organisation: Pioneer Works

Location: Pioneer Works, Red Hook, Brooklyn, New York, USA

Award: Visual Arts: USD 5,000 one-time award (USD 2,500 honorarium + USD 500/month stipend for 5 months). Music: USD 3,000 one-time award (USD 2,500 honorarium + USD 500/month stipend for 1 month). Free studio space and facilities access. No housing or travel provided. No application fee.

Duration: Visual Arts: 5 months. Music: 1 month. Cohort begins after jury decisions in late September 2026.

Deadline: 13 Jul 2026

Pioneer Works in Red Hook, Brooklyn invites applications from emerging visual artists and musicians for its 2027 residency cohort. 12 Visual Arts residents (5-month residency) and 12 Music residents (1-month residency) are selected each year through a two-phase jury process. WHAT IS PROVIDED: free studio space; access to facilities including risograph printer, darkroom, ceramics studio, large-format printer, 3D printers, thermobinder, and laser cutter; biweekly studio visits with curators, gallerists, critics, and artists; free access to all public programmes at Pioneer Works, including Second Sundays (monthly open studios and performances). FINANCIAL AWARD: USD 5,000 for Visual Arts residents; USD 3,000 for Music residents (each comprising a USD 2,500 honorarium plus USD 500/month for the residency duration); applicants' choice on how to spend it. Pioneer Works does not provide housing, travel, or visa sponsorship. ELIGIBILITY: open locally, nationally, and internationally; collectives may apply (resources shared); current students and programme alumni are not eligible. REVIEW PROCESS: phase one is internal scoring on technical ability, uniqueness of vision, and future potential (nontraditional career paths given special consideration); highest scorers advance to an external jury of art professionals and alumni who assess diversity of demographics and media, genuine studio need, and fit with Pioneer Works' public-facing culture. Jury convenes September 2026; all applicants notified by late September. Supported by Dalio Philanthropies, the Andy Warhol Foundation, NYC Department of Cultural Affairs, New York State Council on the Arts, and the National Endowment for the Arts. APPLY: via Pioneer Works website by 13 July 2026. Questions: residency@pioneerworks.org.

Open call details

item.title: Samstag International Visual Arts Scholarships - Class of 2027
item.description:
Organisation: Samstag Museum of Art, University of South Australia

Location: Australia (applicants must be Australian citizens or permanent residents); 12-month placement at an international institution of the scholar's choice.

Award: AUD $75,000 tax-exempt stipend; institutional fees for one academic year of study (where applicable); return airfares; and travel and health insurance. No application fee.

Duration: 12 months overseas at an international learning institution.

Deadline: 30 Jun 2026

The Samstag International Visual Arts Scholarships are annual scholarships enabling Australian artists to develop their artistic capacities through a dedicated year of practice-based learning at an international institution of their choice. AMOUNT: AUD $75,000 tax-exempt stipend; institutional fees for one academic year (where applicable); return airfares; and travel and health insurance. ELIGIBILITY: Australian citizens or permanent residents aged 18 or over who are either (a) currently enrolled in a practice-led visual arts program at an Australian institution of higher education, having successfully completed at least two years of an undergraduate or postgraduate degree; or (b) graduates of a practice-led visual arts undergraduate or postgraduate qualification at an Australian institution of higher education (graduates of more than five years' standing are now eligible following 2023 guideline updates). PROCESS: applicants must create an account in the online portal, download the Samstag Microsoft PowerPoint template, read the Class of 2027 Guidelines, and complete and submit the online application form. Accounts can be created before the deadline and applications saved for later submission. Prior applicants (Classes of 2024, 2025, or 2026) can reuse the same account. Deadline: 30 June 2026, midnight Australian Central Standard Time (ACST). Apply at https://samstag.adelaide.edu.au/scholarship/apply/.

item.title: Small File Media Festival 2026 - Call for Work
item.description:
Organisation: Small File Media Society

Location: Canada; festival screening. Open to international submissions.

Award: No payment stated; festival programming and screening. No submission fee stated.

Duration: One-time festival screening.

Deadline: 01 Jul 2026

The Small File Media Festival 2026 invites moving-image submissions under the theme "Can Small Files Save the World?" -- a call for arthouse, video work, glitch aesthetics, punk, sci-fi, narrative, and documentary work made with a small-file mindset. This year the festival especially welcomes small-scale experiments with narrative and documentary. The festival frames Small File Ecomedia as a political and ecological intervention: small files function in regions with precarious infrastructure, circumvent bandwidth imperialism and surveillance, and expose the environmental cost of mainstream online video. WHAT THEY WANT: craftily composed, elegantly performed, and artfully minimised movies in any genre; use of obsolete technologies, creative compression techniques, and low-bandwidth aesthetics at all stages of production is encouraged. Resources for making small-file work at www.smallfile.ca/make. Open to artists, activists, media pirates, rogue technologists, and filmmakers. No payment or submission fee stated. All submitted information used for festival programming and promotion -- ensure accurate spelling, punctuation, and capitalisation. Submissions close 1 July 2026 at midnight. Submit at https://smallfile.ca/submit/.

Open call details

item.title: The Richell Prize for Emerging Writers 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Emerging Writers' Festival and Hachette Australia

Location: Australia; must currently reside in Australia.

Award: AUD $10,000 prize money (donated by Hachette Australia); 12-month mentorship with a Hachette Australia publisher; first option to consider finished work for publication. Long- and short-listed writers also eligible to apply for a Casa Morphosi Residency in Abruzzo, Italy. No application fee.

Duration: 12-month mentorship.

Deadline: 13 Jul 2026

The Richell Prize for Emerging Writers, presented by the Emerging Writers' Festival and Hachette Australia, is an annual prize for unpublished writers of adult fiction and adult narrative non-fiction. The winner receives AUD $10,000 and a 12-month mentorship with a Hachette Australia publisher, who will work with the writer to develop their manuscript, with first option to consider the finished work for publication. Long- and short-listed writers are also eligible to apply for a Casa Morphosi Residency in Abruzzo, Italy. The prize was established in memory of Hachette Australia CEO Matt Richell (d. 2014). ELIGIBILITY: must currently reside in Australia (Australian citizens, permanent residents, and long-term visa holders residing in Australia; those residing overseas are not eligible); unpublished writers only -- no previous publication by a publishing house (including small press or independent publishers in any genre); publication in anthologies, literary journals, scholarly works, writing PhD projects, or self-publishing does not disqualify. ELIGIBLE GENRES: adult fiction (including short story collections) and adult narrative non-fiction (including memoir and essay collections). Poetry, scripts, plays, picture books, practical non-fiction, and works for children or young adults are not eligible. SUBMISSION FORMAT: single PDF comprising the first three chapters (maximum 20,000 words), a one-page synopsis, and a chapter breakdown of up to two A4 pages; plus a statement (up to 750 words) on how winning the prize will further your writing career, entered in the online form. Manuscript must be complete or intended to be completed; does not need to be finished at time of submission. A4, double-spaced, 12pt Roman font (e.g. Garamond or Times New Roman), 3cm margins, page numbers. TIMELINE: prize opened 23 April 2026; closes 13 July 2026 at 11:59pm AEST; longlist announced 11 September 2026; shortlist 16 October 2026; winner announced 6 November 2026. Administered with support from Simpsons Solicitors. Apply at https://emergingwritersfestival.org.au/richell-prize-2026/.

Open call details

Location: Tampere, Finland (multiple simultaneous venues, site-specific)

Award: Technical support provided. No artist fee publicly confirmed. No application fee.

Duration: Festival date: 5 September 2026.

Deadline: 30 Jun 2026

Siirtymä Ambient Music Festival 2026 is a free, community-driven festival in Tampere, Finland, organised by Suomen Ambientyhdistys ry (Finnish Ambient Association). The festival uses overlapping simultaneous performances across multiple locations, emphasising spatial and ambient experience over traditional stage formats. Open to artists and collectives working in ambient music, sound art, spatial installations, and interdisciplinary practices; no career-stage restriction; the festival prioritises emerging and lesser-known artists. The 2026 theme focuses on strengthening diversity, creating multiple voices, and finding new ways to encounter one another and the urban environment. Technical support is provided to selected participants. No travel or accommodation funding is publicly confirmed. NOTE: deadline of June 30 is from the Community Arts Network PDF; verify at https://siirtyma.com/en/siirtym-2026 before submitting. Contact Suomen Ambientyhdistys ry directly for application details.

item.title: GovAI UK Winter Fellowship 2027 - Research Track
item.description:
Organisation: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)

Location: London, UK; in-person, full-time.

Award: £12,000 salary for the three-month term, plus travel support to London and weekday lunch provided. Visa sponsorship (3-month temporary work visa) available. No application fee.

Duration: 3 months, 18 January to 9 April 2027.

Deadline: 12 Jul 2026

GovAI's UK Winter Fellowship (Research Track) is a three-month in-person fellowship in London for researchers and policy professionals seeking to enter or advance careers in AI governance and policy. Fellows conduct independent research on a topic of their choice, paired with a GovAI supervisor from the team or network; the first two weeks are spent exploring the AI governance landscape before a research proposal is finalised. Research outputs may include a report, white paper, journal article, op-ed, or blog post targeted at an audience relevant to AI governance. Alongside the project, fellows attend expert Q&As, weekly seminars, skills workshops, work-in-progress meetings, and social events, and are encouraged to discuss follow-on career opportunities with GovAI's team and network. GovAI alumni have gone on to roles in US, UK, and EU government; leading AI companies (Anthropic, DeepMind, OpenAI); top think tanks (CSET, RAND); and leading universities (Oxford, Cambridge). AMOUNT: £12,000 salary for three months, plus travel support to London and weekday lunch. Visa sponsorship for a 3-month temporary work visa is available; successful visa applicants must remain in their country of application while the visa is processed. ELIGIBILITY: open to a wide range of backgrounds (government, academia, industry, civil society); no strict degree requirements; relevant areas include public policy, political science, computer science, economics, biosecurity, cybersecurity, China studies, and risk management. Senior candidates who need accommodations should email fellowships@governance.ai with subject line 'senior candidate winter fellowship 2027' and a CV. SELECTION CRITERIA: quality and clarity of written work, relevant expertise, judgement in prioritising research directions, and team fit (intellectual honesty, openness to feedback, comfort with uncertainty). APPLICATION PROCESS: (1) written application and automated abstract reasoning assessment; (2) paid remote work test (August 2026); (3) remote interview and reference checks (September 2026); decisions communicated October 2026. Deadline: 12 July 2026, 23:59 BST.

item.title: Claude Corps Fellowship
item.description:

Location: USA; in-person at host nonprofit organizations across America (placement location varies; relocation support available).

Award: $85,000 full-time annual salary plus benefits, expansive Claude token budget, and relocation support as needed. No application fee.

Duration: 12 months.

Deadline: 17 Jul 2026

Claude Corps is a national fellowship program from Anthropic placing early-career fellows full-time at nonprofits across America for one year, with the goal of extending AI's benefits to communities and building fellows' AI skills. Anthropic funds the program and provides Claude expertise; CodePath (America's largest collegiate CS education provider) is the fellows' official employer of record and leads programming; Social Finance leads measurement and evaluation. AMOUNT: $85,000 full-time salary plus benefits; expansive Claude token budget; relocation support as needed. FELLOW EXPERIENCE: intensive Claude training at the start; five hours of ongoing weekly training throughout; support from a CodePath mentor; Anthropic office hours for technical questions; and professional guidance from the host organization manager. SAMPLE HOST ORGANIZATIONS: Braven (Chicago), Code the Dream (Durham NC), Heartland Forward (Bentonville AR), Montgomery County Food Bank (Conroe TX), Reef Environmental Education Foundation (Key Largo FL), StriveTogether (Cincinnati), YMCA of Greater Charlotte, and more than 400 nonprofits total. ELIGIBILITY: anyone over 18 with under two years of full-time work experience, regardless of educational background; must be authorized to work in the US; must be comfortable working with Claude and willing to relocate if necessary. COHORTS: Cohort 1 (100 fellows, starts October 2026) -- applications close 17 July 2026; Cohort 2 (starts January 2027) and Cohort 3 (starts August 2027) -- rolling applications. Apply at https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-corps.

item.title: Scaling AI Safety for a Multi-Agent World (Joint Funding Call)
item.description:
Organisation: Schmidt Sciences, Google DeepMind, the Advanced Research & Invention Agency (ARIA), the Cooperative AI Foundation, and Google.org

Location: Global; open worldwide, with collaborations across geographic boundaries encouraged.

Award: Two funding tiers (applicants may apply to either or both), projects 1 to 2 years. Tier 1: up to USD 300,000 for exploratory research, pilot studies, or focused technical investigations. Tier 2: USD 300,000 to USD 1,000,000 for more ambitious or collaborative projects. For projects funded by Schmidt Sciences, indirect costs must be at or below 10%. No application fee.

Duration: 1 to 2 years

Deadline: 08 Aug 2026

A joint philanthropic funding call by Schmidt Sciences, Google DeepMind, ARIA, the Cooperative AI Foundation and Google.org to catalyse the foundational scientific research needed to understand, evaluate and control risks emerging from large-scale ecosystems of interacting AI agents deployed by multiple actors (multi-principal, multi-agent settings). The call argues that focusing only on the safety and alignment of individual models is insufficient, and seeks system-level approaches. RESEARCH AGENDA (four clusters): (1) Sandboxes and Testbeds, realistic, reproducible multi-agent environments for studying populations of frontier-model agents; (2) The Science of Agent Networks, how collective capabilities emerge and scale, how networks fail, and how dangerous population-level properties can be detected; (3) Strengthening Agent Infrastructure, evaluating and stress-testing primitives such as identity, verifiability, reputation, communication and commitment; (4) Multi-Agent Oversight and Control, detection, attribution, security and intervention methods to keep deployed agent populations safe at scale. Proposals may target one cluster or span several, with priority on depth over breadth and on realistic sandboxes and testbeds. OUT OF SCOPE: single-agent safety, capability advancement without a clear safety motivation, toy systems, naive application of pre-existing solutions, commercial product development, and purely conceptual/policy work. ELIGIBILITY: individual researchers, research teams, research institutions and multi-institution collaborations across universities, national laboratories, institutes and nonprofit research organisations; open globally. Informational webinars on 30 June and 23 July 2026; notification of decisions in autumn 2026. Proposals due 8 August 2026 by 11:59pm Anywhere on Earth. Contact: multiagentsafety@schmidtsciences.org. Apply at https://schmidtsciences.smapply.io/prog/scaling_ai_safety_for_a_multi_agent_world/.

Open call details

item.title: Rodger and Joann McLennan Canadian War Art Research Fellowship 2026
item.description:
Organisation: National Gallery of Canada

Location: Ottawa, Canada; tenable only at the National Gallery of Canada.

Award: Up to $5,000 (including expenses and/or stipend). No application fee.

Duration: One calendar year, beginning 1 September 2026.

Deadline: 26 Jun 2026

The National Gallery of Canada's Rodger and Joann McLennan Canadian War Art Research Fellowship encourages and supports advanced research in Canadian and Indigenous Canadian war and military art. Research must relate to any aspect of Canadian war or military art (including painting, drawing, sculpture, photography, printmaking, and other media) and must emphasise the use and investigation of the collections of the National Gallery of Canada, the Canadian War Museum, and Library and Archives Canada. The Fellowship supports original research by scholars undertaking publishing, exhibition, thesis, and dissertation work or their equivalents. AMOUNT: up to $5,000, including expenses and/or stipend. TENURE: one calendar year beginning 1 September 2026; tenable only at the National Gallery of Canada. ELIGIBILITY: open to art historians, curators, critics, conservators, graduate students, and independent or other professionals working in the visual arts, museology, and related disciplines in the humanities and social sciences; international applicants welcome. Researchers from outside Canada must ensure all visa requirements and documentation are in order before entry and the start of the Fellowship. Applications are invited from all qualified candidates, including Indigenous peoples, women, people of any sexual orientation, gender identity or gender expression, racialized people, and people with disabilities. TO APPLY: submit a complete application in English or French as a single PDF by email to Amy Rose, Senior Manager, Library, Archives and Research Fellowship Program, at erefel@gallery.ca. Deadline: 26 June 2026.

item.title: MAK Center: Artists and Architects in Residence 2027/2028 (MAK Schindler Scholarship, Los Angeles)
item.description:
Organisation: MAK Center for Art and Architecture, Los Angeles

Location: Los Angeles, California, United States (Mackey Apartments). Residents must be present in L.A. for the full six-month term.

Award: Six-month MAK Schindler Scholarship residency with accommodation provided at the Mackey Apartments. The open call does not state a stipend, travel or honorarium figure; confirm any additional financial support directly with the MAK Center. No application fee stated.

Duration: Six months. Residency terms run October to March or April to September; 2027/2028 cycle.

Deadline: 12 Aug 2026

The MAK Center for Art and Architecture awards Artists and Architects in Residence scholarships (the MAK Schindler Scholarship) to independent projects exploring the relation between art and architecture. Projects are chosen for their ability to critically examine current trends in art, architecture and society; to make a compelling connection with Los Angeles as the place of implementing or continuing the applicant's own work; and to demonstrate a conceptual and experimental approach. Emphasis is placed on the L.A. location, and residents' projects often reflect, incorporate and address specific facets of the city and its social fabric. Recipients are encouraged to take part in the MAK Center's activities and to attend meetings, workshops and project discussions with other artists and students. ELIGIBILITY: freelance artists; fine arts or architecture students at a university who have completed the '2. Studienabschnitt'; or architects or artists who have recently graduated. Teams of up to three are eligible if all members practice together as an artist group or collective of architects, are all present in L.A. for the six months, and name a contact responsible for the team. HOUSING: the Mackey Apartments are made available to scholarship holders only; family members, relatives and friends may be accommodated for a maximum of two weeks during the term, permitted in advance by Beth Stryker, Director of the MAK Center. Eligibility is excluded if the applicant has been or will be granted another scholarship in the USA immediately before or after the MAK scholarship, or if the data form is incomplete or the required portfolio is missing. Residency terms run October to March and April to September. Deadline 12 August 2026. Apply: https://www.makcenter.org/apply-mackey

item.title: Fluxus Experimental Film & Media Arts Festival 2026: Call for Submissions
item.description:
Organisation: Hamilton Artists Inc., in partnership with McMaster University School of the Arts and Factory Media Centre

Location: Hamilton, Ontario, Canada; submitting artists must be currently based and/or working in Canada.

Award: Free to submit (NO entry fee). IMAA-scale artist remuneration (a screening/exhibition fee following the Independent Media Arts Alliance fee schedule) is paid to successful applicants within 30 days of the screenings.

Duration: Two screenings, 18 and 19 September 2026

Deadline: 15 Jul 2026

Fluxus is a contemporary short film and media arts festival hosted in partnership between Factory Media Centre, Hamilton Artists Inc and McMaster University School of the Arts. Its mission is to provide an accessible forum for the exhibition of creative, experimental, moving-image art forms, and to develop connections between Canadian media artists, arts institutions and the public. Fluxus showcases experimental and boundary-pushing media works from artists of all levels working in Canada; submissions are free and unrestricted by theme, open to a spectrum of artforms including but not limited to animation, documentary, video art, collage film, music video and experimental film. The festival takes place over two screenings at Hamilton Artists Inc on Friday 18 and Saturday 19 September 2026 (the Friday screening is dedicated to emerging media artists and filmmakers); all screenings are free to attend. REMUNERATION: there is no submission fee, and IMAA-scale artist remuneration is paid to successful applicants within 30 days of the screenings. GUIDELINES: artists must be currently based and/or working in Canada; work must be short format (15 minutes or less) and no more than 5 years old (2021 or later); submissions are digital Vimeo or YouTube links (private/password-protected accepted if the password is provided); solo artists, groups, collectives and collaborative works are welcome, with a limit of one submission per artist/group/collective; films must be independent projects over which the artist retains full creative and artistic control. AI POLICY: Fluxus discourages the use of generative AI in submitted works; if AI factors into the creative process, its use must be declared and described (see Factory Media Centre's Policy on the Use of Generative AI). APPLY via the online submission form with an artist bio (75 words max), artist CV (PDF, 3 pages max), a brief description of the work (100 words max), entry information, the video link, and two image stills. Notification is expected in August; due to the high quantity of submissions, only selected artists will be contacted. Fluxus is committed to equity and strongly encourages applications from Black and Indigenous artists, persons with disabilities, ethnic or racial minorities, immigrants and refugees, Francophones, LGBTQI+ persons, women, and persons of all socio-economic backgrounds. Deadline: Wednesday 15 July 2026 at 11:59pm EST. Questions: programming@theinc.ca.

Open call details

item.title: Frame Fellowship 2.0 (Residential Accelerator for AI Safety Content Creators) 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Frame Fellowship

Location: San Francisco, USA; residential (in person).

Award: USD 10,000 stipend for the 10 weeks, plus accommodation, meals and flights covered. Access to production-ready studios in San Francisco, a mentor and creator network, and connections to funding, sponsorship and partnership opportunities at organizations such as FLI, CAIS and Seismic. Top performers may be eligible for additional grant opportunities beyond the program. No application fee.

Duration: 10-week residential program

Deadline: 10 Jul 2026

Frame Fellowship is a 10-week residential accelerator in San Francisco for content creators educating the public on AI safety. Frame's premise is that content creators can solve a long-standing bottleneck of expanding reach and trust beyond the AI safety and effective altruism (EA) bubble; creators today hack attention, empower mass action and shape culture. Past Frame Fellows include award-winning filmmakers, journalists from leading newsrooms and ex-activists who transitioned to educating, empowering and preparing their communities for the impacts of AGI. Frame helps amplify their impact to millions of people, connects them to funding and sponsorship opportunities at organizations such as FLI, CAIS and Seismic, and helps establish their path as a leading content creator. This cohort runs 10 weeks with a stronger mentor and creator network, access to production-ready studios in San Francisco, housing, food and a stipend, plus connections to funding and partnership opportunities after the program. TWO TRACKS: (1) Independent Track, build your own channel and become a defining voice on a topic; (2) Amplifier Track, embed with an AI org/company and grow their reach. SUPPORT: Frame covers accommodation, meals, flights and a USD 10,000 stipend for the 10 weeks so fellows can go all in; top performers may be eligible for additional grant opportunities beyond the program.
